coredata.dc:title: Intestinal delivery of ROS-scavenging carbonized polymer dots for full-course treatment of acute and chronic radiation enteritis
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Jiaxin
Yang, Mingxi
Tang, Xiaoduo
Li, Shuang
Zhang, Junhu
Lu, Laijin
Wang, Quan
Yang, Bai
coredata.dc:description: Radiation enteritis is an unavoidable complication of radiotherapy for pelvic tumors that seriously affects the prognosis of cancer patients. The fundamental mechanism inducing cell death and tissue injury is the generation of excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) by ionizing radiation. In the present study, we developed novel S-doped red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (S-rCPDs) with high biocompatibility and efficient ROS-scavenging capability to act as an intestinal protective agent against radiation damage. The S-rCPDs significantly eliminates cellular ROS and alleviates DNA damage under X-ray radiation. To achieve drug accumulation within the distal intestine, S-rCPDs loaded pH-controlled release polymer microspheres (S-rCPDs@MPs) is produced via microfluidic technology. In vivo experimental results show that oral administration of S-rCPDs@MPs effectively suppresses the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines and alleviates acute radiation intestinal injury, with a better protective effect than amifostine, which is hereto the only clinically approved radioprotector. Moreover, the as-prepared S-rCPDs@MPs could stabilize the balance of intestinal flora and continuously downregulate the expression of transforming growth factor β1 (TGF-β1), eventually inhibiting the progression of chronic intestinal fibrosis. This study provides a novel strategy for whole-course treatment of radiation enteritis using CPDs-based materials, which could inspire the development of other radioprotective nano-drugs.

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: S-rCPDs protects intestinal cells from radiation-induced damage in vitro
S-rCPDs@MPs mitigates the acute radiation-induced intestinal injury
S-rCPDs@MPs suppresses inflammatory cytokine storm and promotes DNA repair
S-rCPDs@MPs maintains stability of intestinal flora

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The preparation of microspheres on microfluidic chip was done by Xiaoduo Tang and Junhu Zhang. This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NSFC; under Grant Nos. 82002498 , 22035001 , 21774041 ), Development Center for Medical Science & Technology National Health Commission of the People's Republic of China (WA2021RW19), Department of Finance of Jilin province ( JLSWSRCZX2020-0016 ), Department of Science and Technology of Jilin province ( 20200802010GH ), and JLU Science and Technology Innovative Research Team ( 2017TD-06 ), Interdisciplinary Fund of the First Hospital of Jilin University (JDYYJCHX2020007). Some icons in Fig. 2 , 3 , and 4 were created with BioRender.com.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthetic procedures and mechanism of intestinal radioprotection. (a) Schematic illustration of the development of S-rCPDs@MPs. (b) Oral delivery of S-rCPDs@MPs to distal intestine, where they collapse and release S-rCPDs. (c1) S-rCPDs scavenges ROS and promotes DNA repair to reduce DNA damage. (c2) S-rCPDs inhibits NF-κB expression to control the inflammatory cytokine storm and decrease intestinal injury. (c3) S-rCPDs stabilizes the balance of gut microbiota, downregulates TGF-β1 expression, and block intestinal fibrosis.

Characterization of S-rCPDs and S-rCPDs@MPs. (a) TEM images of S-rCPDs (insets show high-resolution-TEM images and size distribution of S-rCPDs). (b) Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of S-rCPDs under different excitation wavelengths (insets show images of S-rCPDs solutions under daylight and green laser flashlight irradiation). (c) UV-vis absorption, optimal excitation, and emission PL spectra of S-rCPDs. (d) FT-IR spectrum of S-rCPDs. (e) XPS analysis of S-rCPDs (inset shows deconvoluted S 2p spectra). (f) ESR spectra of DEMPO/•OH adducts of different samples. (g) Schematic diagram of the microfluidic preparation procedure for S-rCPDs@MPs. (h) Fluorescence microscopy images of S-rCPDs@MPs (left: bright-field, right: 543 nm excitation) (scale bar: 200 μm). (i) In vivo intestinal fluorescence imaging of mice after oral administration of S-rCPDs@MPs (left) and S-rCPDs (right) at different times.

Radioprotective effect of S-rCPDs on IEC-6 cells. (a) Cytotoxicity assays of S-rCPDs with IEC-6 cells. (b) Cell viability of IEC-6 cells under X-ray irradiation. (c) Relative colony numbers of IEC-6 cells under X-ray irradiation. (d) Calcein/PI double staining of IEC6 cells after different treatments. Scale bar: 100 μm. (e) ROS detection using DCFH-DA. Scale bar: 25 μm. (f) DNA damage detection using Comet assay. Scale bar: 50 μm. (g) ROS levels recorded with a fluorescence microplate reader. (h) Tail length of DNA with different treatments. (i) S-rCPDs is in close proximity to DNA to achieve effective protection by scavenging ROS nearby.

S-rCPDs@MPs prevents the acute intestine damage induced by X-ray irradiation. (a) Experimental design. Mice were treated with S-rCPDs@MPs, S-rCPDs, or amifostine and then received X-ray irradiation. Mice were sacrificed 4 h, 3 days, and 6 days after X-ray irradiation. (b) Body weights of mice under X-ray irradiation with different treatments. Error bars represent mean ± SD (n = 6). (c) DAI scores observed and recorded after X-ray irradiation. Error bars: mean ± SD (n = 6). (d) H&E staining of distal small intestine of mice under X-ray irradiation with different treatments. Scale bar: 100 µm. (e) TUNEL staining of distal small intestine. Scale bar: 100 µm. (f) Assessment of mesenteric blood flow 6 days after X-ray irradiation. Cold colors indicate low flow; warmer colors higher flow. (g) Villus height of distal small intestine with different treatments at day 6 after X-ray. (h) Quantitation of TUNEL+ cells. (i) Quantitation of mesenteric blood flow. (j) Survival rates of mice under 16 Gy X-ray irradiation with different treatments (n = 6).

S-rCPDs@MPs inhibits cytokine storm induced by X-ray irradiation. (a) Volcano plot of DEGs between X-ray versus control (left) and S-rCPDs@MPs +X-ray versus control (right) groups. (b) GSEA of KEGG gene sets of mouse distal small intestine with or without X-ray treatment. The top 15 enriched gene sets after X-ray irradiation are shown in the bar plot. (c) Heat map of transcription of cytokines (> two-fold, P < 0.05) from the control, S-rCPDs@MPs +X-ray, and X-ray groups. (d) Representative immunohistochemical staining of NF-κB, IL-1β, and CD68. Scale bar: 100 µm. (e) GSEA plots for gene sets NER, MMR, and BER in S-rCPDs@MPs +X-ray versus control groups. (f) Schematic illustration of S-rCPDs@MPs suppressing cytokine storm and promoting repair of damaged DNA.

S-rCPDs@MPs suppresses chronic intestinal fibrosis. (a) Experimental design. Mice were treated with S-rCPDs@MPs, S-rCPDs, or amifostine and then received X-ray irradiation. Mice were sacrificed 3 days, 6 weeks, and 12 weeks after X-ray irradiation. (b) Body weights of mice 12 weeks after X-ray treatment. Error bars: mean ± SD (n = 6). (c) Representative H&E and Masson staining of the distal small intestine 12 weeks after X-ray irradiation. Scale bar: 100 µm. (d) Quantitation of the average thickness of the submucosa of the distal small intestine. (n = 6). (e) Changes in mRNA expression of TGF-β1 in different groups. (f) Representative immunohistochemical staining of TGF-β1 in the distal small intestine 12 weeks after X-ray irradiation. Scale bar: 50 µm.

S-rCPDs@MPs regulated gut microbiota. (a) Principal component analysis of fecal microbiomes (n = 6). (b) Relative abundance of microbial compositional profiling at the family level (n = 6). (c) Linear discriminant analysis effect size of gut microbiota of mice shown by cladogram. The circle of radiation from the inside to the outside represents the classification level from phylum to genus. (d) LDA to identify taxonomic biomarkers between two groups (log10 LDA >3.0).

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Radiation enteritis is an unavoidable complication of radiotherapy for pelvic tumors that seriously affects the prognosis of cancer patients. The fundamental mechanism inducing cell death and tissue injury is the generation of excessive reactive oxygen species (ROS) by ionizing radiation. In the present study, we developed novel S-doped red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (S-rCPDs) with high biocompatibility and efficient ROS-scavenging capability to act as an intestinal protective agent against radiation damage. The S-rCPDs significantly eliminates cellular ROS and alleviates DNA damage under X-ray radiation. To achieve drug accumulation within the distal intestine, S-rCPDs loaded pH-controlled release polymer microspheres (S-rCPDs@MPs) is produced via microfluidic technology. In vivo experimental results show that oral administration of S-rCPDs@MPs effectively suppresses the secretion of pro-inflammatory cytokines and alleviates acute radiation intestinal injury, with a better protective effect than amifostine, which is hereto the only clinically approved radioprotector. Moreover, the as-prepared S-rCPDs@MPs could stabilize the balance of intestinal flora and continuously downregulate the expression of transforming growth factor β1 (TGF-β1), eventually inhibiting the progression of chronic intestinal fibrosis. This study provides a novel strategy for whole-course treatment of radiation enteritis using CPDs-based materials, which could inspire the development of other radioprotective nano-drugs.

ce:section.ce:para: Radiotherapy is frequently used in the treatment of pelvic malignant tumors, including anal, cervical, prostate, and rectal tumors [1]. Although three-dimensional conformal and intensity-modulated radiotherapy have been used clinically, healthy organs adjacent to the tumor are unavoidably irradiated, resulting in radiation-induced diseases [2]. The intestine, as the second most radiation-sensitive organ in the body, is particularly susceptible to damage during pelvic radiation owing to its position in the abdominal cavity [3]. More than 75% patients who receive pelvic radiotherapy show radiation enteritis, which markedly affects their quality of life and may even be life-threatening [3,4]. Generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by irradiation has a significant role in radiotoxicity [5]. ROS can damage DNA, causing cell death [2], subsequently induce cytokine overexpression, resulting in acute intestinal damage (< 3 months) [6], and gradually lead to intestinal fibrosis due to long-term inflammation (> 3 months) [7]. Based on the anti-ROS mechanism, amifostine was developed for radiation diseases and remains the only radioprotector approved by the US Food and Drug Administration. However, the half-life of amifostine is particularly short, and it has no curative effect against chronic intestinal damage induced by irradiation. Besides, amifostine has systemic toxicity and can cause complications even at low doses, which limits its high-dose use [8]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to develop a safe and effective radioprotective agent for whole-course treatment of radiation enteritis.

In recent years, goal-oriented design of nanomaterials has attracted increasing attention owing to the wide range of applications of such materials in disease diagnosis and treatment. Compared with traditional drugs, nanomedicines may have superior characteristics such as better chemical stability, fewer side effects, a more convenient route of administration, and longer half-life [9,10]. Previous studies have shown that carbon-based nanomaterials [11] such as fullerene [12] and carbon nanotubes [13] possess ROS-scavenging abilities. Xie et al. reported that carbon nanoparticles suspension injection [14] and graphdiyne [15] were capable of protecting the intestine from radiation-induced injury through scavenging ROS. However, recent studies have mainly focused on the prevention of acute intestinal injury and paid little attention to treatment of chronic fibrosis; in addition, the lack of luminescent properties of these materials is not conducive to drug tracking.

Carbonized polymer dots (CPDs), which refer to the carbon dots (CDs) that composed of carbon cores and connected polymer chains, are a new type of fluorescent carbon-based nanomaterials [16,17]. Owing to their superior photoluminescence properties, excellent water solubility, and facile preparation and modification, they have become the most promising substitute in the field of biomedicine [18–20]. Recently, there have been reports on the use of doped carbon dots with ROS-scavenging capability for reducing oxidative stress damage to cells and tissues, including alleviating cisplatin-induced acute kidney injury [21], preventing lipopolysaccharide-induced macrophages inflammation [22], and other applications [23–25], hence indicating the excellent biocompatibility and potential therapeutic applications of these new nano-antioxidants. However, the utilization of CPDs in the treatment of radiation enteritis has not been reported.

Herein, for the first time, we developed S-doped red fluorescent carbonized polymer dots (S-rCPDs) to serve as a novel ROS-scavenger for effective intestinal radioprotection (Scheme 1 ). In vitro experiments demonstrated that the S-rCPDs had extremely low toxicity and could significantly eliminate radiation-generated ROS and prevent DNA damage, thereby reducing radiation-induced cell death. To successfully deliver S-rCPDs to the distal intestine, we used a commercial pH-responsive polymer to produce S-rCPDs loaded microspheres (S-rCPDs@MPs) in a microfluidic chip. Experiments in animal models of radiation enteritis showed that S-rCPDs@MPs significantly mitigated acute radiation-induced intestinal injury by inhibiting oxidative stress damage and controlling inflammatory cytokine storm, with a better efficacy than amifostine. Moreover, S-rCPDs@MPs could stabilize the gut flora and continuously downregulate the expression of TGF-β1, eventually suppressing radiation-induced intestinal fibrosis. The present study provides a whole-course treatment for radiation enteritis using biocompatible CPDs, which can be developed as candidate intestinal radioprotective agents.

Citric acid anhydrous and glutathione (reduced) were purchased from Aladdin. Formamide was purchased from Beijing Reagent. pH-responseive polymer EUDRAGIT L 30 D-55 was purchased from EUDRAGIT®. H2O2 was purchased from Tianjin Reagent. Deionized water was produced by Milli-Q and used during all the experiments. IEC-6 cells (a normal rat small intestine epithelial cell line) were purchased from Shanghai Biological Technology Co., Ltd. Dulbecco's modified Eagle medium (DMEM) was purchased from Gibco company. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was purchased from Biological Industries. Amifostine was purchased from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The CCK-8 Kit, Calcein/PI Live/Dead Viability/Cytotoxicity Assay Kit, ROS Assay Kit, and TUNEL Apoptosis Assay Kit were purchased from Beyotime Biotechnology. The Comet assay kit was from Trevigen Company. 4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole (DAPI) was purchased from Life Technologies. TRIzol reagent was purchased from Sigma-Aldrich. The primary antibodies including NF-κB, IL-1β, CD68, and TGF-β1 were purchased from Abcam. The DAB Horseradish Peroxidase Color Development Kit was purchased from Dako. Avertin was purchased from Sigma.

The S-rCPDs was synthesized by a one-step solvothermal method. Briefly, 1 mmol citric acid and 2 mmol glutathione were dissolved in 10 mL formamide and then transferred to a 25 mL poly(tetrafluoroethylene) Teflon lined autoclave, followed by heating in an oven at 180°C for 4 h. When the reactor was cooled to room temperature, the dark liquid in the kettle was poured into 40 mL acetone and left to settle overnight. The mixture was centrifuged at a speed of 10,000 rpm for 10 min, after which the precipitate was collected and further washed with 20 mL acetone three times. The solid at the bottom of the tube was redispersed in water and dialysis (500-1000 Da) against water for 24 h. The S-rCPDs were finally obtained by lyophilization.

For the preparation of S-rCPDs@MPs, following the classical production methods, the microfluidic droplet chip was fabricated according to the following scheme. The silicon mold was prepared by conventional photolithography and inductively coupled plasma dry etching, and the PDMS microchannels were prepared by soft lithography. The PDMS microchannels and the underlying glass were both bonded together after 5 min of oxygen plasma treatment. We first prepared a EUDRAGIT L 30 D-55 solution with various mass fraction of S-rCPDs (1, 2.5, and 5 mg/ml); the aqueous phase (a EUDRAGIT L 30 D-55 solution containing S-rCPDs) and the oil phase (5 wt% Span80 and 0.5% acetic acid in mineral oil) were injected into the inlets of the microfluidic device by syringes. The droplets flowed from the outlet and into the mineral oil at pH = 3.5, adjusted by acetic acid, to make the microspheres solidify through the connected plastic tube. After being fully cured for 30 min, the S-rCPDs@MPs was thoroughly washed with a buffer solution (pH = 4) before use.

High-resolution TEM images were photographed on Hitachi JEOL JEM-2100 F. Photoluminescence (PL) and UV-vis absorption spectra were recorded on Shimadzu RF-5301 PC and 3100 UV-vis spectrophotometers, respectively. FT-IR spectra was measured on Nicolet Avatar 360FTIR spectrophotometer. XPS was performed on ESCALAB 250 spectrometer with mono X-ray source Al Kα excitation (1486.6 eV). ESR spectra was determined on JEOR JES−FA200.

IEC-6 cells were cultivated in DMEM with 1% penicillin and streptomycin and 10% FBS. The cells were cultivated in an incubator at a temperature of 37°C with 5% CO2. The medium was changed every 2 days to ensure sufficient nutrients for the cells. CCK-8 assay was applied to assess the cytotoxicity of S-rCPDs to IEC6 cells using a CCK-8 kit. In short, cells were inoculated in 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well for 24 h. After attachment, the medium was changed with new media with a series of concentrations of S-rCPDs. After culture for 2 days, cells were washed twice with PBS, followed by addition of 10% CCK-8 solution and incubation for another 1 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Finally, the absorbance was determined with a microplate reader (Biotek Epoch) at 450 nm.

IEC-6 cells were inoculated for 1 day in confocal dishes at a density of 1 × 104 cells/well. Afterwards, cells were cultivated with S-rCPDs (200 µg/mL) for 0.5, 1, 2, 4, 8, and 24 h at 37°C. After each incubation, the cells were washed twice with PBS and stained using DAPI. Finally, a confocal laser scanning microscope was used to record fluorescence images of the cells.
Cells and animals were irradiated with X-ray irradiator (Rad Source Technologies, Inc., Alpharetta, GA) (1.5 Gy/min).

CCK-8 assay was chosen to assess the damage caused by X-ray to IEC6 cells. In brief, cells were inoculated in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well for 1 day. After attachment, the cells were treated by X-ray irradiation at various doses (4, 8, 12 16, and 20 Gy). After culturing for 48 h, cells were washed twice with PBS, followed by addition of 10% CCK-8 solution and incubation for another 1 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Finally, a microplate reader (Biotek Epoch) was used to determine the absorbance at 450 nm.

IEC-6 cells were inoculated in a 96-well plate at a density of 5 × 103 cells/well for 24 h. After attachment, 1 mg/mL of S-rCPDs or 0.3 mg/mL of amifostine or 1 mg/mL of glutathione was used to treat the cells for 2 h; the cells were subsequently exposed to a 12 Gy dose of X-ray irradiation. After 2 days, cells were washed twice with PBS, followed by addition of 10% CCK-8 solution and incubation for another 1 h (37 °C, 5% CO2). Finally, the absorbance was detected with a microplate reader (Biotek Epoch) at 450 nm.

For the calcein/PI assay, the CCK-8 solution was replaced with a calcein/PI solution (1 μL/mL calcein: 1 μL/mL PI) solution to detect cell viability. The cells were examined using a fluorescence microscope to determine dead and live cells with 515–560 nm and 450–490 nm band-pass excitation filters.

IEC-6 cells were inoculated in a six-well plate at a density of 200 cells/well and cultivated for 24 h. Then, the cells were incubated with 1 mg/mL of S-rCPDs or 0.3 mg/mL of amifostine or 1 mg/mL of glutathione, followed by X-ray irradiation (12 Gy). The medium was changed every 2 days. Nine days after irradiation, methanol was used to fix the cells, which were then stained with crystal violet (0.5%). Colonies containing >50 cells were collected as survivors. The relative colony number was calculated as the number of colonies generated/number of cells inoculated.

Single-cell gel electrophoresis assay was used to assess damaged DNA in cells in accordance with the manufacturer's instructions. Briefly, IEC6 cells were cultivated with 1 mg/mL of S-rCPDs or 0.3 mg/mL of amifostine or 1 mg/mL of glutathione for 2 h, followed by X-ray irradiation (12 Gy). After 48 h, the cells were collected and suspended at 1 × 105/mL in PBS. Molten LMAgarose was mixed with the cells (at a temperature of 37°C) with a ratio of 10: 1 (v/v), and the mixture was spread immediately on CometSlide™ slides. The glass slides were soaked in lysis solution for 2 h at 4°C and subsequently kept in freshly prepared alkaline solution (EDTA: 500 mM, NaOH: 8 g/L, in dH2O) in the dark for 20 min at 25°C. The slides were maintained in an electrophoresis slide tray containing 850 mL of 4°C alkaline electrophoresis solution. Electrophoresis was conducted at 25 V for 30 min. SYBR® Green solution was placed on each agarose circle, followed by staining in the dark for 10 min. Finally, slides were evaluated using a fluorescence microscope (Nikon, Japan).

IEC-6 cells were inoculated in confocal dishes at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well. After attachment, cells were incubated with 1 mg/mL of S-rCPDs or 0.3 mg/mL of amifostine or 1 mg/mL of glutathione for 2 h and subsequently exposed to X-ray irradiation (12 Gy). After 24 h, the cells were stained with 1 mL of DCFH-DA (10 μmol/L) for 20 min at 37°C. Then, cells were washed twice with PBS. Finally, a confocal laser scanning microscope was used to record fluorescence images of the cells. For microplate reader assay, IEC-6 cells were inoculated in 96-well plates and their fluorescence was assessed with a microplate reader.

C57BL/6 female mice, 8–10 weeks old, and female athymic nude mice, 4–6 weeks old, were purchased from Beijing Wei Tong Li Hua Laboratory Animal Technology Co., Ltd., Beijing, China. All mice were placed in a specific-pathogen-free animal facility (at a temperature range of 22 ± 2°C, with 55 ± 10% humidity, and a 12–12 h light–dark cycle) and received sterilized food and water. All the animal procedures in the current study were authorized by the Animal Ethics Committee of the First hospital of Jilin University (20210279).

Briefly, S-rCPDs@MPs (100 mg/kg) was administered to mice by oral gavage, S-rCPDs (100 mg/kg) was administered to mice by oral gavage or tail vein injection. The mice were sacrificed at preset time points (0.5, 4, 8, 16, 24, and 48 h) after administration. Then, the major organs were harvested and washed briefly with PBS. Finally, an IVIS optical imaging system was used to record fluorescence images.

To establish a stable total abdominal irradiation (TAI) model, avertin (800 mg/kg by intraperitoneal injection) was used to anesthetize mice, and modified lead plates were used to shield each mouse's body except the abdomen during X-ray irradiation. TAI at 12 and 16 Gy was chosen for the in vivo experiments [26,27]. Amifostine (150 mg/kg) was used as a positive control and was administered intraperitoneally 30 min before TAI. PBS, S-rCPDs (100 mg/kg), and S-rCPDs@MPs (100 mg/kg) were administered orally by gavage 6 h before TAI. Survival condition, body weight, vitality, and defecation of mice were observed, and tissues were gathered at the designated time points for in-depth analysis. The intestinal tissues at 3 and 6 days after TAI were used to assess the acute radiation damage and 12 weeks after TAI were used to evaluate the chronic radiation damage.

Clinical symptoms were assessed using the DAI based on stool blood volume, stool consistency, and weight loss. The DAI was scored in accordance with the scoring system previously described [28]. All the mouse groups were monitored every 2 days after X-ray treatment to evaluate the treatment effect and disease process.

LSCI was implemented to monitor blood flow in the mesenteric vasculature as described in a previous report [29]. Briefly, mice were anesthetized with avertin (800 mg/kg by intraperitoneal injection) and laid on their sides. After forming a small midline incision, a part of the distal small intestine was placed externally with the aim of exposing the mesenteric vascular system. The perfusion images were captured using custom software (RWD Life Science). Body temperatures of mice were maintained at 37 ± 0.5°C throughout the experimental process.

Total RNA from intestinal tissues was harvested using TRIzol reagent. cDNA synthesis was conducted with an RT EasyTM II (with gDNase) kit. RT-qPCR was performed using SYBR Green and a LightCycler 480 system. The delta-delta Ct method was applied to determine the relative fold change of gene expression. TGF-β1 primer: forward: 5′- TTGCTTCAGCTCCACAGAGA-3′, reverse: 5′- GTTGGACAACTGCTCCACCT-3′. β-actin primer: forward: 5′- AGGCCAACCGTGAAAAGATG-3′, reverse: 5′- AGAGCATAGCCCTCGTAGATGG-3′.

The distal small intestine (5–10 cm proximal to the cecum) was dissected from mice, washed in ice-cold PBS, and fixed in formalin solution (4%) for 1 day. Next, the fixed tissues were dehydrated in a series of graded ethanol concentrations and then embedded in paraffin; 5 μm paraffin sections were used for Masson, H&E, IHC, and TUNEL staining. For IHC, sections were incubated with primary antibodies at 4 °C overnight, followed by incubation with secondary antibodies. The DAB Horseradish Peroxidase Color Development Kit was used for color reaction in IHC staining. TUNEL staining was performed with a One Step TUNEL Apoptosis Assay kit (green fluorescence). Images were taken with a laser scanning microscope (Nikon).

Feces of mice were collected from the cecum 4 weeks after X-ray treatment and stored at -80 °C for microbiota analysis. Bacterial 16S rRNA sequencing was conducted by OE Biotech, Inc. (Shanghai, China). A DNA Extraction Kit (MagPure Soil DNA LQ Kit, D6356-02) was used for extraction of total genomic DNA. Primers 798R (5′-AGGGTATCTAATCCT-3′) and 343F (5′-TACGGRAGGCAGCAG-3′) were used to amplify the V3–V4 variable regions in 16s rDNA genes. Subsequently, in the FastQ format, the original sequencing data were pretreated with Trimmomatic software [30] and assembled with FLASH software [31]. Finally, QIIME software [32] was used to retain reads with 75% of bases exceeding Q20, and operational taxonomic units with similarity cutoff of 97% were produced with Vsearch software [33].

The transcriptome sequencing was conducted by OE Biotech, Inc. (Shanghai, China). Total RNA of distal intestine tissue samples from the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray, control, and X-ray groups was separated, enriched, and purified using oligo-dT magnetic beads. Libraries were constructed using a TruSeq Stranded mRNA LTSample Prep Kit (Illumina, San Diego, CA, USA) according to the manufacturer's instructions. The libraries were then sequenced on an Illumina platform (Illumina HiSeq X Ten or HiSeqTM 2500) and the 125 bp/150 bp paired-end reads were obtained. Afterwards, raw data (raw reads) were treated with Trimmomatic [30] in order to acquire clean reads, which were mapped to the reference genome with hisat2 [34]. A P value less than 0.05 and fold change less than 0.5 or greater than 2 were considered to indicate significantly differential gene expression. The GSEA v.4.1.0 software was used for analysis of functional KEGG pathways based on gene sets from MSigDB v.7.4; a nominal false discovery rate less than 0.25 and P value less than 0.05 were regarded as significant.

A total of 24 female athymic nude mice (from 4 to 6 weeks of age) were used in the xenotransplantation assays. The right abdominal flank region was injected subcutaneously with 2 × 106 HCT116 cells in PBS. Tumor volumes were measured every 2 days according to the formula: V = Dshort 2 × Dlong÷2. Mice were randomly classified into four groups (PBS, S-rCPDs@MPs, PBS + X-ray, and S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray) when the mean tumor volume reached 80–100 mm3. Tumors received X-ray (5 Gy/fraction) every 2 days for a total of four times, and the other parts of the mice were shielded with lead plates. S-rCPDs@MPs were administered by gavage 6 h before X-ray treatment. At the end point, the tumors were captured and weighed.

Statistical computations were performed by using GraphPad Prism 8.0 (GraphPad, San Diego, CA, USA). Quantitative data were described as mean ± standard deviation. The log-rank test was performed to analyze mouse survival rates. The statistical significance between two groups was evaluated with unpaired Student's t-tests. P-value less than 0.05 was regarded as statistically significant. (*P < 0.05, **P < 0.01, ***P < 0.001, ****P < 0.0001; ns, not statistically significant)

The S-rCPDs was prepared by the solvothermal method utilizing citric acid and glutathione as precursor. As shown in Fig. 1 a, S-rCPDs presented uniformly dispersed spherical morphology with an average diameter of 2.2 nm under transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and the high-resolution TEM image represented lattice fringes of 0.21 nm, corresponding to (100) facet of graphite carbon (Fig. 1a inset) [25]. The S-rCPDs exhibited deep red to near-infrared fluorescence, possessing an excitation-independent emission wavelength at 682 nm with a tailing peak around 750 nm (Fig. 1b). The absolute quantum yield (QY) of S-rCPDs was 10.47%, such long-wavelength fluorescence was conducive to penetrating deep tissues for in-vivo bio-imaging and drug tracking. The ultraviolet-visible (UV-vis) absorption spectra of S-rCPDs contained several peaks at 250-450 nm and 550-700 nm (Fig. 1c), which could be attributed to the π→π* transition of C=C bonds in the carbon core and n→π* transition of C=O/C=N/C=S bonds on the surface, respectively [35]. To investigate the chemical structure and composition of S-rCPDs, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was performed; the results showed the stretching vibration of O-H/N-H at the broad peaks around 3180 cm−1, and vibration of C=O at 1684 cm−1 and C=N at 1396 cm−1 (Fig. 1d). Moreover, the peak at 1084 cm−1 and 1158 cm−1 could be ascribed to the stretching vibrations of C=S and C-S bonds, respectively, indicating the surface modification of sulfur-containing functional groups [36]. The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis also confirmed the N and S doping within S-rCPDs (Fig. 1e and Fig. S1); the deconvoluted S 2p spectra revealed four peaks at 162.6, 164.5, 165.6, and 168.8 eV, which could be ascribed as thiolate, S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 of C-S, and oxidized S=O bonds, respectively (Fig. 1e inset) [37]. It is believed that the S-doping endow CPDs with high reducing activity, which means that S-rCPDs can serve as effective antioxidants. The free-radical-scavenging activity of S-rCPDs was further assessed by electron spin-resonance spectroscopy (ESR) (Fig. 1f). The signal with representative 1: 2: 2: 1 peak intensity illustrated the hydroxyl radical (•OH) from the decomposition of H2O2 captured by DEMPO, while the peaks almost disappeared when S-rCPDs was introduced, suggesting the strong •OH-scavenging capability of S-rCPDs [38]. It is well known that ROS generated by ionizing radiation can lead to the occurrence of oxidative stress in vivo, inducing inflammation and lesions. Therefore, the S-rCPDs might be capable of functioning as a protective agent to suppress tissue damage caused by radiation.

Oral administration represents a powerful strategy in the treatment of intestinal disease [10,39], and accumulation of a drug at the target site is beneficial for its therapeutic effect [10]. The distal intestine is relatively close to the pelvic cavity and susceptible to radiation damage after pelvic radiotherapy. To increase S-rCPDs’ therapeutic efficiency, it needs to be successfully delivered to the distal intestine. The pH values along the gastrointestinal tract vary from pH 1–3 in the stomach to pH 7.4 in the terminal ileum [10]. Thus, a protective shell that is stable under acidic conditions and released under neutral conditions may meet the requirements. We utilized a commercial pH-sensitive polymer EUDRAGIT L 30 D-55 (polymer segment structure shown in Fig. S2) as coatings for the preparation of S-rCPDs loaded pH-controlled release microspheres (S-rCPDs@MPs) on a three-channel microfluidic chip (Fig. 1g and Fig. S3a). The polymer, which has modified poly(meth)acrylate as its main component, gradually dissolves above pH 5.5, which ensures that the S-rCPDs@MPs remains stable through the stomach and releases S-rCPDs in the distal intestine. Fluorescence microscopy images revealed uniform microspheres with average diameter of 130 μm; the size distribution could be made very narrow and adjusted by varying the flow rate of the syringe pump (Fig. S3b,c). Benefiting from the superior compatibility of S-rCPDs, the composite MPs exhibited bright and stable red photoluminescent emission (Fig. 1h), which could be exploited for real-time drug tracking in vivo. After post-cleaning treatment, the S-rCPDs encapsulation concentration could reach 3.9 mg/mL when the initial S-CPDs-containing polymer precursor solution concentration was 5 mg/mL. An in vitro release test based on fluorescence intensity showed that S-rCPDs@MPs remained relatively stable at low pH and collapsed when the pH rose from 2 to 5.5 (Fig. S4), which satisfied our requirement of drug release in the distal intestinal tract. As shown in Fig. 1i, 30 min after oral administration of the S-rCPDs and S-rCPDs@MPs to mice, the fluorescence intensity markedly increased in the gastrointestinal tract, demonstrating the continuous accumulation of S-rCPDs and S-rCPDs@MPs in the intestine. However, at 8 and 16 h after oral administration, there was a significant difference in fluorescence intensity distribution between S-rCPDs and S-rCPDs@MPs. Compared with S-rCPDs, S-rCPDs@MPs was retained for a longer time in the distal intestinal tract, thereby increasing the S-rCPDs exposure in the distal intestine. Moreover, fluorescence was seen in the heart, lung, liver, spleen, and kidney after oral gavage. S-rCPDs@MPs showed stronger fluorescence signals in these organs than S-rCPDs at 8, 16, and 24 h after oral administration (Fig. S5a). We could also see that oral administration of S-rCPDs had a longer circulation time compared to intravenous administration, and S-rCPDs was rapidly excreted by kidneys (Fig. S5). Therefore, orally administrated S-rCPDs@MPs improved bioavailability and could provide potential continuous protection against radiation damage.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-rCPDs protects intestinal cells from radiation-induced damage in vitro
ce:section.ce:para: Ionizing radiation generate excessive ROS in living organisms, which cause cell damage [2]; hoverer, some agents with good ROS-scavenging ability have not shown resistance to radiation injury [40]. Therefore, it was reasonable to explore the radioprotection ability of S-rCPDs in a cell model. Here, we used the rat small intestinal crypt epithelial cell line (IEC-6) to evaluate the radioprotective ability of S-rCPDs [14]. First, the cytotoxicity of S-rCPDs was assessed by Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) assay. The cell-safe concentration of S-rCPDs was defined as the concentration at which more than 90% of the cells remained viable. According to the cell viability analysis, S-rCPDs was not toxic to IEC-6 cells at concentrations up to 2 mg/mL (Fig. 2 a). Next, the cellular uptake of S-rCPDs was evaluated by confocal fluorescence microscopy, the cytoplasm and nucleus exhibited an increasing fluorescence signal over time (Fig. S6). The above results demonstrated that S-rCPDs had low toxicity and could be efficiently taken up by cells. Then, a 12-Gy dose of X-ray radiation was chosen for the subsequent in vitro radioprotective experiments (Fig. S7), and amifostine at a concentration of 0.3 mg/mL was used as a positive control [41]. As shown in Fig. 2b, the viability of IEC-6 cells in the S-rCPDs + X-ray group (81.3%) showed a significant increase compared with those in the amifostine + X-ray group (65.8%), and the viability in the glutathione + X-ray group (43.2%) was similar to that in the X-ray group (41.2%). Consistent results were obtained from a colony formation assay (Fig. 2c and Fig. S8) and calcein/propidium iodide (PI) assay (Fig. 2d). These results provided strong evidence that S-rCPDs possessed protective effects against radiation-induced cell damage.

High ROS directly cause DNA damage, subsequently leading to cell injury [42,43]. Thus, a possible mechanism by which S-rCPDs protected IEC-6 cells involved scavenging ROS and preventing oxidative stress damage to DNA. To test our hypothesis, a 2,7-dichlorodi-hydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) fluorescent probe was used to detect ROS levels in cells. As shown in Fig. 2e, the fluorescence significantly increased after exposure to X-ray irradiation, and S-rCPDs could effectively inhibit fluorescence generation in cells. The quantitative measurements of ROS in cells showed consistent results (Fig. 2g). These findings demonstrated the excellent ability of S-rCPDs to scavenge ROS. Next, we conducted single-cell gel electrophoresis (Comet assay) to evaluate the DNA damage in cells [44]. The results showed that the cells in the X-ray group had long tails (representing damaged DNA) (Fig. 2f), whereas cells treated with amifostine had shorter tails than those treated with X-ray alone. In contrast, cells treated with S-rCPDs had negligible tails (Fig. 2f). The quantitative analyses of damaged DNA further verified these results (Fig. 2h). The above results confirmed that S-rCPDs could effectively protect DNA from X-ray-induced damage.

It was worth mentioning that glutathione (the precursor of S-rCPDs) reduced ROS levels after irradiation but did not suppress DNA damage (Fig. 2e-h). It has been reported that a protective compound must be in close proximity to DNA to achieve effective protection [45,46]; cationic mercaptans agglomerate near DNA, whereas anionic mercaptans avoid the vicinity of DNA [45,46]. Glutathione has a slightly negative charge and is not a good radioprotector [45,46]. However, our experiments showed that the S-rCPDs had a positive charge and was able to enter the cell nuclei (Fig. S6 & S9). We assumed that this might contribute to the excellent radioprotective performance of S-rCPDs (Fig. 2i).

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-rCPDs@MPs mitigates the acute radiation-induced intestinal injury
ce:section.ce:para: Prior to the in vivo experiments, we examined the biological safety of S-rCPDs@MPs in mice. As shown in Fig. S10a, the body weight curves of mice from the S-rCPDs@MPs and control groups were almost identical. The hematology and biochemistry analysis (Fig. S10b-d) also showed no significant differences between the two groups. In addition, hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) staining revealed no sign of structural damage of lung, liver, spleen, kidney, or heart tissues (Fig. S11) in either group, demonstrating the biosafety of S-rCPDs@MPs.

Next, the radioprotective ability of S-rCPDs@MPs during the acute stage after X-ray irradiation was evaluated in vivo. Previous studies showed that a 12 Gy dose of TAI would not cause mortality and was suitable for investigation of the intestinal damage resulting from X-ray irradiation [26,27]. We chose amifostine (150 mg/kg body weight) as the positive control to assess the therapeutic effect of S-rCPDs@MPs [47]. As shown in Fig. 3 a, compared with the S-rCPDs + X-ray and amifostine + X-ray groups, the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group showed higher body weights (Fig. 3b) and lower DAI (Fig. 3c). In order to further verify the radioprotective effect of S-rCPDs@MPs on the distal intestine, we performed pathological analysis of the distal small intestine. H&E staining of tissues from the X-ray group showed that the small intestinal villi were severely injured on day 6 after TAI (Fig. 3d,g). The villi of mice treated with amifostine and S-rCPDs presented less damaged than those in the X-ray group; however, those in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group remained long and intact, and were nearly the same as those in the control group (Fig. 3d,g), indicating that S-rCPDs@MPs could mitigate acute radiation-induced intestinal injury. Further, TUNEL staining was performed to evaluate apoptosis in the distal small intestinal crypts; the results showed that S-rCPDs@MPs could suppress apoptosis induced by X-ray irradiation (Fig. 3e,h). Some studies found that vascular endothelial cell damage and subsequent ischemia are key factors contributing to intestinal damage induced by X-ray [26,48]. Laser speckle blood flow imaging results showed that the mesenteric blood flow in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group was significantly higher than those in other groups receiving TAI (Fig. 3f,i). Finally, a 16 Gy X-ray TAI was applied to explore the effects of S-rCPDs@MPs on the survival of mice [26]. As shown in Fig. 3j, the S-rCPDs@MPs+X-ray group exhibited the lowest mortality rate among all groups receiving TAI. All these results indicated that S-rCPDs@MPs could effectively protect the distal small intestine from irradiation-induced acute injury, and that its protective efficiency was superior to that of uncoated S-rCPDs or the clinical drug amifostine.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-rCPDs@MPs suppresses inflammatory cytokine storm and promotes DNA repair
ce:section.ce:para: To reveal the molecular mechanism by which S-rCPDs@MPs protects the small intestine from radiation damage, we conducted mRNA sequencing from the distal small intestine three days post TAI. As illustrated in the volcano plots in Fig. 4 a, the S-rCPDs@MPs could significantly reverse the gene expression induced by X-ray treatment. In total, there were 4219 differentially expressed genes (DEGs) (absolute fold change ≥1.5 and p ≤ 0.05) between the X-ray and control groups; however, only 2432 DEGs were found between the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray and control groups (Fig. S12a). We further performed gene set enrichment analysis (GSEA); the top 15 enriched Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) gene sets after irradiation were presented in Fig. 4b. The gene set associated with cytokines and cytokine receptor interactions (cytokines) was the most enriched in the X-ray group; Nod-like receptor signaling pathway (NLR) and Toll-like receptor signaling pathway (TLR) were also enriched. It has been reported that damaged DNA could activate NF-κB transcription by NLR and TLR to induce the overexpression of various cytokine genes, leading to a pro-inflammatory cytokine storm and causing tissue damage [49]. However, GSEA among the three groups showed that the gene sets related to cytokines, NLR, and TLR that were strongly upregulated in the X-ray group were not enriched in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group (Fig. S12b), and heat map analysis further illustrated that S-rCPDs@MPs reversed the overexpression of cytokines, NLR, and TLR resulting from X-ray irradiation (Fig. 4c and Fig. S12c,d). Immunohistochemical (IHC) staining of NF-κB, IL-1β, and CD68 verified the RNA sequencing results (Fig. 4d). These results indicated that S-rCPDs@MPs inhibited acute radiation damage by controlling the cytokine storm.

Then, we examined the results of the GSEA comparing the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray and X-ray groups. Nucleotide excision repair (NER), mismatch repair (MMR), base excision repair (BER), and DNA replication pathways were enriched in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group (Fig. 4e and Fig. S12e). NER, MMR, and BER are involved in DNA-damage repair[50]. These results demonstrated that S-rCPDs@MPs not only downregulated the expression of NLR, TLR, and NF-κB, suppressing the cytokine storm, but also promoted repair of damaged DNA (Fig. 4f).

Radiation-induced intestinal fibrosis is a chronic adverse effect of abdominal radiation therapy that frequently occurs months or years after irradiation [3,27]. We explored whether S-rCPDs@MPs could protect the intestine from fibrosis induced by X-ray irradiation (Fig. 5 a). The anti-fibrosis effects of each treatment (amifostine, S-rCPDs, S-rCPDs@MPs) were evaluated 12 weeks after X-ray irradiation. As shown in Fig. 5b, compared with the S-rCPDs + X-ray and amifostine + X-ray groups, the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group showed higher body weights. H&E and Masson staining (Fig. 5c,d) showed that the distal small intestine in the X-ray group had typical morphological features, as indicated by fibrotic submucosa thickening and hyperplasia, whereas hardly any characteristics of damage were observed in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group. The above results confirmed that S-rCPDs@MPs were able to block the progression of chronic small intestinal fibrosis.

Next, we explored how S-rCPDs@MPs affected the process of fibrosis. Transforming growth factor β (TGF-β) is a multifunctional profibrogenic cytokine [7], and TGF-β1 has been shown to promote the differentiation of myofibroblasts, resulting in the production of collagen [51]. The changes in mRNA expression levels of TGF-β1 were quantified by reverse-transcriptase quantitative PCR (RT-qPCR). As shown in Fig. 5e and Fig. S13, the mRNA expression levels of TGF-β1 gradually increased after radiation. The TGF-β1 expression levels in the amifostine + X-ray and S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray groups were similar on day 3 after irradiation. Subsequently, however, the TGF-β1 expression level gradually increased in the amifostine + X-ray group, whereas that in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group remained low for 12 weeks. These results were verified by IHC staining of TGF-β1 (Fig. 5f). These results indicated that S-rCPDs@MPs inhibited small intestinal fibrosis through TGF-β1 signaling.

ce:section.ce:section-title: S-rCPDs@MPs maintains stability of intestinal flora
ce:section.ce:para: It is a well-known fact that there is an association between the intestinal flora and various chronic gastrointestinal diseases, including intestinal fibrosis [52]. Because S-rCPDs@MPs is administered orally, we hypothesized that it may protect the intestine from fibrosis by regulating the gut microbiota. To investigate whether S-rCPDs@MPs could affect the composition of intestinal flora, the feces of mice were collected from the cecum 4 weeks after X-ray treatment for microbiome analysis using 16S rRNA sequencing. Principal component analysis (Fig. 6 a) indicated that X-ray irradiation significantly changed the composition of the bacterial community in comparison with the control group, consistent with the results of other studies [53–55]. The composition of microflora in the two treatment groups was similar to that in the control group; however, the intra-group differences in intestinal flora in the S-rCPDs@MPs + X-ray group were much smaller than those in the S-rCPDs + X-ray group. The results demonstrated that S-rCPDs@MPs was more effective than S-rCPDs in maintaining the microbiota composition, probably owing to its’ intestinal controlled release and long-term retention. The enterobacterial composition of each sample in the four groups was calculated at the flora family and genus levels. The results, which are illustrated as bar charts and heatmaps in Fig. 6b and Fig. S14, confirmed that S-rCPDs@MPs could preserve the stability of intestinal flora better than S-rCPDs. We also performed linear discriminant analysis (LDA) to determine the taxa that were differentially abundant among the different groups (Fig. 6c, d); LDA score exhibited the dominant groups and their impact at various levels from phylum to genus. All the above results indicated that S-rCPDs@MPs could effectively maintain the composition of gut microbiota in mice to inhibit the development of intestinal fibrosis.

It is essential that a radioprotective agent has no effect on tumor growth and efficacy of radiation therapy. Based on this, we conducted tumor-related experiments (Fig. S15a). Local X-ray irradiation (5 Gy each time) of tumors was performed four times, once every 2 days, and S-rCPDs@MPs was administered 6 h before each X-ray irradiation. The tumor growth curves showed no difference between the control and S-rCPDs@MPs groups, or between the X-ray and S-rCPDs@MPs+X-ray groups (Fig. S15b,c), demonstrating that the administration of S-rCPDs@MPs did not promote tumor growth or negatively affect the efficacy of radiotherapy. The tumor weight statistics also confirmed these results (Fig. S15d). In summary, the as-prepared S-rCPDs@MPs with ROS-scavenging capability could effectively alleviate acute radiation intestinal injury and was also capable of stabilizing the intestinal flora to inhibit chronic intestinal fibrosis. The present work provides an oral-enteral delivery method for full-course treatment of radiation enteritis, showing potential to become a novel candidate of radioprotective agents.

We developed S-rCPDs as a protective agent against radiation-induced enteritis for the first time. Benefiting from excellent biocompatibility and ROS scavenging capability, the S-rCPDs could reduce radiation-generated cellular ROS levels and prevent DNA damage. To optimize the efficacy with oral administration, pH-controlled-release S-rCPDs@MPs were prepared by microfluidic chip technology. In vivo experimental results showed that S-rCPDs@MPs accumulated more than S-rCPDs in the distal intestine of mice and significantly reduced acute intestinal injury caused by irradiation through inhibiting the inflammatory cytokine storm. The therapeutic effect was even better than that of amifostine, the only clinically approved medicine for radiation protection. Moreover, S-rCPDs@MPs could stabilize the intestinal flora, downregulate the expression of TGF-β1, and eventually decrease chronic intestinal fibrosis. Our study provides a versatile nanoplatform for oral administration of treatments for acute and chronic radiation enteritis, which may inspire the development of other nano-drugs for intestinal diseases.
